summary: I have in my possession one of the duelling pistols used by Aaron Burr in his infamous duel with Alexander Hamilton. Burr was left-handed, and this pistol was designed for his left hand, which made it unique. It was Burr who fired the shot that mortally wounded Hamilton. These pistols were made by U. W. Richards and are of the flint-rock type, a remarkable piece of history that my family somehow came to own.

Alexander Hamilton was the man who died as a result of the duel with Aaron Burr, a tragic event that left a lasting mark on history. The pistols used in that duel belonged to Hamilton, and they were exactly alike except for the handedness. Hamilton was an Englishman, and the pistols were made by U. W. Richards, a fine manufacturer. The duel and its consequences are a story I have often reflected on, especially since one of those pistols is in my family’s possession.

U. W. Richards was the manufacturer of the duelling pistols that played a significant role in American history. These pistols, made of the finest steel with mahogany handles, were flint-rock type and crafted with great skill. The name U. W. Richards is engraved on the pistol I have, linking this maker to the duel between Aaron Burr and Alexander Hamilton. It’s fascinating to hold such a finely made piece from that era, knowing its historical significance.

I was born in New College on the Franklin campus of the University of Georgia, a place with deep family ties. My grandfather was one of the builders of New College, and during the war when the university was closed, he and his family lived there. It holds a special place in my life story, marking the beginning of my journey in Athens, Georgia.

Athens, Georgia, is where I was raised and spent my childhood with my five brothers. Our home was on a street that was once part of the old stage route to Dahlonega, winding among the trees. My father was instrumental in starting the public school system here, and we had many adventures swimming in local branches and playing pranks. Athens is truly the backdrop of my early life and family history.

Dahlonega, Georgia, was a significant place for my family because my grandfather had gold mining interests there. It was a two-day trip from Athens back then, and the journey involved a spring wagon and trusted servants. Years later, I took my mother on a trip to Dahlonega and back in one day, something she never thought possible. Dahlonega represents both family enterprise and cherished memories.

Jefferson, Georgia, was a stopping point on the way to Dahlonega during family trips. Our trusted servant would spend the first night there before moving on to Sugar Hill. It was about 20 miles from Athens and part of the route my family took for their mining interests. Jefferson is a small but important part of those journeys I remember well.

Sugar Hill, Georgia, was another key stop on the way to Dahlonega for my family. The servant would usually reach Sugar Hill on the second night of the trip, where our family carriage would often catch up with the wagon. It was part of the routine when traveling to oversee grandfather’s gold mining operations. Sugar Hill holds a place in the story of those long journeys.

One of my grandfather’s sons was a commissioner in charge of building the State Capitol in Atlanta. Remarkably, it is the only State Capitol in the United States built within its appropriation, with a balance of $5.60 left after completion. This fact has always impressed me and speaks to the dedication and skill involved in that project. The Capitol stands as a proud family legacy connected to public service and craftsmanship.

Professor Orr from Martin Institute was hired as a teacher in the school my father established for me and my brothers. Our school grew so popular that it eventually caused the private school run by our mother’s friend to close. Martin Institute’s connection through Professor Orr was part of the quality education we received during our childhood in Athens. It was a significant institution in our early learning experience.

The Civil War had a profound impact on my family and community. During the war, the University of Georgia was closed, and my grandfather’s family lived in New College. My father served in the war and later hired Professor Hudson, a fellow soldier, to tutor me and my brothers. The war also affected local infrastructure, like when lead pipes were taken up to be made into bullets. The Civil War shaped much of the environment I grew up in.

Watkinsville, Georgia, is where Dr. Billups, a fine old dentist, practiced. After his death, my father bought his dental kit and gave it to me, which became my first tool for earning money. I remember treating a neighbor’s toothache with oil of cloves from that kit and earning my first nickel. Watkinsville is connected to my early ambitions and the start of my nickname 'Doc.'
